name: Tail Call Elimination
summary: Rewrites a call in tail position — where the caller does nothing after the callee returns — into a jump, reusing the current stack frame. Mandatory for languages relying on recursion for iteration (Scheme, OCaml, Erlang); LLVM exposes it via `musttail` (strict) and `tail` (opportunistic). WebAssembly added tail calls in 2023.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Tail call elimination (TCE) rewrites a call in tail position — where the caller does nothing after the callee returns — into a jump, reusing the current stack frame. This is mandatory for languages that rely on recursion for iteration: Scheme's "proper tail calls", OCaml, Erlang, Haskell in practice. Without TCE, an iterative algorithm written as recursion would exhaust the stack.

The mechanical transformation: after argument evaluation, overwrite the caller's arguments in place, deallocate locals, and `jmp` to the callee instead of `call`. The result is constant-stack-space tail recursion.

sections.title: Implementation and ABI hazards
sections.content:
LLVM exposes TCE via the `musttail` attribute (strict correctness — the call *must* be a tail call, error otherwise) and the `tail` hint (opportunistic — the optimiser may convert if profitable). Most production languages use `musttail` for language-mandated tail calls (Scheme's proper-tail-call requirement) and rely on `tail` for opportunistic backend conversion of accidentally-tail-positioned calls.

The implementation hazard is ABI compatibility. Tail calls interact awkwardly with ABIs that expect callee-cleanup or with stack-walking debuggers. Most C ABIs technically permit TCE but many legacy tooling assumptions (stack traces, unwinding, profilers) break if it happens. Languages that need reliable TCE either define it at the IR level (LLVM `musttail`, WebAssembly tail calls) or compile to trampolines when the target ABI is hostile.

sections.title: WebAssembly tail calls
sections.content:
WebAssembly added tail calls in 2023 as the `return_call` and `return_call_indirect` instructions. The proposal (Andreas Rossberg et al.) was motivated by language compilers (Scheme, OCaml, Haskell) targeting Wasm needing reliable proper-tail-call semantics that don't depend on host-environment ABI cooperation.

The Wasm semantics are clean: `return_call f` evaluates the callee's arguments, deallocates the current frame, and transfers control to `f` as if `f` were called from the current function's caller. Stack space is bounded; arbitrary tail recursion compiles correctly. The same compositional property — predictable cross-language tail calls without ABI variance — that LLVM `musttail` provides for native code, Wasm now provides for browser-and-runtime deployment.
